records.ety: [F. sollicier, L. sollicitare, solicitare, -atum, fr. sollicitus wholly (i. e., violently) moved; sollus whole + citus, p. p. of ciere to move, excite. See Solemn, Cite.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Solicited; p. pr. & vb. n. Soliciting.]

defs: To ask from with earnestness; to make petition to; to apply to for obtaining something; as, to solicit person for alms.
quotes: Did I solicit thee From darkness to promote me?

defs: To endeavor to obtain; to seek; to plead for; as, to solicit an office; to solicit a favor.
quotes: I view my crime, but kindle at the view, Repent old pleasures, and solicit new.

defs: To awake or excite to action; to rouse desire in; to summon; to appeal to; to invite.
quotes: That fruit . . . solicited her longing eye.
Sounds and some tangible qualities solicit their proper senses, and force an entrance to the mind.

defs: To urge the claims of; to plead; to act as solicitor for or with reference to.
quotes: Should My brother henceforth study to forget The vow that he hath made thee, I would ever Solicit thy deserts.

defs: To disturb; to disquiet; -- a Latinism rarely used.
quotes: Hath any ill solicited thine ears?
But anxious fears solicit my weak breast.
